summary: I was born in Ibaraki Prefecture in 1891 and stayed there until I was about four years old. My mother was also from Ibaraki, and although she came from a rich family, we lived in poverty due to my father's limited income as a pastor. I have fond memories of my early childhood there before we moved due to my father's assignments.

We lived in Yamanashi Prefecture for the longest time during my childhood after leaving Ibaraki. It was very cold there in winter, and my father, as a Methodist pastor, would walk 25 miles in the snow to attend another church once a week, often returning home around 1 or 2 a.m. I attended high school there after studying in Tokyo, and those years deeply influenced my desire to become a missionary.

Our family moved to Shizuoka Prefecture after Yamanashi as part of my father's pastoral assignments. Although I don't recall many specific details about our time there, it was one of the several places we lived during my early years before settling in Chiba. These moves were all part of my father's work as a Methodist pastor.

I attended grammar school in Nagano Prefecture, where I experienced my father's strict discipline firsthand. I remember once complaining about my lunch, and my father became so angry that he threw my lunch box down the stairs. It was a tough time, but it shaped my understanding of his devotion and strict Methodist upbringing.

Chiba Prefecture was the last place we lived in Japan before I left for America. After I was married, I returned to Chiba with my two oldest children and stayed with my mother for a month. She took good care of her grandchildren, and saying goodbye to her then was very emotional, as it was the last time I saw her before she passed away.

My father was a devout Methodist, which greatly influenced my upbringing. We had worship services before every meal, and my father's devotion inspired me to become an evangelist. I later worked in various Methodist churches in Japan and America, including the Pine United Methodist Church in San Francisco, where I helped many picture brides.
cues: church work in Japan and America

summary: Dr. McDonald was a Canadian missionary who came to Tokyo and introduced my father to Christianity. It was through Dr. McDonald's influence that my father converted and later decided to become a pastor. His role was pivotal in shaping my father's life and, consequently, my own path in the church.

Tokyo was where my father initially worked as a newspaperman before becoming a pastor. I attended Toyo Eiwa Girls' High School in Azabu, Tokyo, for four years before moving to Yamanashi. Later, I was assigned to Mita Methodist Church in Tokyo, where I worked before moving to the United States.

My younger brother went to school in Chicago after almost graduating from college in Japan, but sadly he died of tuberculosis while there. His death was one of several tragedies in our family due to poverty and illness. Chicago represents a place of both hope and sorrow for our family.

I attended Toyo Eiwa Girls' High School in Azabu, Tokyo, for four years before moving to Yamanashi. This school was part of my early education and helped prepare me for my later decision to become a missionary. It was an important foundation in my life.

After graduating from high school in Yamanashi, I attended a Bible school in Yokohama to prepare for missionary work. Yokohama was where I received my theological training before being appointed to churches in Nagoya and Toyohashi. It was a significant place in my spiritual and professional development.

My first church appointment after graduating from Bible school was in Nagoya, where I worked for about a year. This was my initial experience as a church worker and missionary. It was a stepping stone before moving to Toyohashi for a longer assignment.

I was assigned to a church in Toyohashi after Nagoya and worked there as a church worker for five years. This period was important for gaining experience and serving the community. It was part of my journey before moving to Tokyo and later to the United States.

I came to Los Angeles to help with the many picture brides who were having difficulties adjusting to life here. I worked there for about a year and a half alongside Ms. Hiraoka, assisting young brides who often faced abuse and hardship. It was a challenging but meaningful time in my ministry.

After Los Angeles, I was sent to San Francisco to work at the Pine United Methodist Church for two years. There were many picture brides there as well, and I lived in the Women's Home run by the Missionary Society, helping children of divorced parents and supporting the brides. San Francisco was where I met my husband, Mr. Imai.

I worked at the Pine United Methodist Church in San Francisco for two years, where I helped many picture brides and their children. The church was a center for support and community, and I lived in the Women's Home affiliated with the Missionary Society. This church was also where I met my future husband through mutual connections.

Ms. Hiraoka was another church worker who came with me to Los Angeles to help with the picture brides. We worked together for about a year and a half, but since the church only needed one woman worker, I later moved to San Francisco. She was a colleague and companion during that challenging time.

I worked closely with picture brides in Los Angeles and San Francisco during the early 1920s. Many of these young women were married to much older men they had never met, leading to tragic situations including abuse and divorce. I visited their homes, helped them find support, and worked with ministers to grant divorces when necessary. It was difficult work, but many brides endured and eventually improved their lives.

I recall one picture bride who ran away from her husband and came to the Women's Home in San Francisco from Napa, California. Her husband came after her, which led to a scary situation where the pastor had to call a lawyer and the police intervened to protect her. Napa was part of the wider area where these difficult cases occurred.

I met Mr. Imai through the Pine United Methodist Church in San Francisco, introduced by his friend Mr. Endo. We married in 1923, the year of the Great Kanto earthquake in Tokyo. He had been a pastor in Walnut Grove since 1915, working hard to abolish gambling and improve the community. Our marriage was a love marriage, and we shared a commitment to church work and social reform.

Mr. Endo was a member of the Pine Methodist Church and a friend of Mr. Imai. He introduced me to Mr. Imai, which eventually led to our marriage in 1923. He played a key role in connecting us during my time working in San Francisco.

My marriage to Mr. Imai took place in 1923, the same year as the Great Kantō earthquake in Tokyo. Although I was in America at the time, the earthquake was a significant event that marked that year for us. It was a time of great upheaval in Japan while we were starting our life together abroad.

Mr. Imai ministered in Walnut Grove starting in 1915, a place that was then a dirty Chinese gambling town. He worked hard to abolish gambling by bringing the Salvation Army band to march and singing hymns through Chinatown and Japantown. Despite threats and near violence, he persisted in his efforts to improve the community. I joined him there about seven years after he arrived and stayed for seven years.

The Salvation Army band was brought by Mr. Imai to Walnut Grove to help march against gambling. Their presence was part of the anti-gambling campaign in a town known for vice and violence. This collaboration was instrumental in raising awareness and challenging the gambling culture there.

In Stockton, California, Mr. Imai led Sunday school children in anti-gambling marches through Chinatown and Japantown, carrying banners and singing hymns. The town was rough, and a man was even killed by gamblers during that time. These events showed the dangerous environment in which Mr. Imai worked to reform the community.

Chinatown in Stockton was one of the places where Mr. Imai led anti-gambling marches with Sunday school children. It was part of a rough area with gambling and violence, and the marches were a bold effort to bring change. The community there was challenging but important in our mission work.

Japantown in Stockton was another area targeted during the anti-gambling marches led by Mr. Imai and the Sunday school children. Along with Chinatown, it was a place where gambling was prevalent, and our efforts aimed to bring moral reform through public demonstrations and hymn singing.

Dr. Saburo Akamatsu was a member of our church in Walnut Grove who later became an evangelist. Before leaving for college in the East, we held a farewell party for him, which was a moving event. His decision to become an evangelist was inspired during that gathering, and he is now ill in New York City.

Dr. Saburo Akamatsu, who was once a member of our Walnut Grove church, is now ill in New York City. He had gone there after deciding to become an evangelist, a decision made during a farewell party we held for him. New York represents the place where his journey took him after leaving our community.

Mr. Nagawa was the father of a Sunday school pupil and attended the farewell party for Dr. Akamatsu in Walnut Grove. He came drunk but was moved to tears by Dr. Akamatsu's testimony about becoming an evangelist. His emotional response was a significant moment witnessed by many at the party.

Dinuba, California, is mentioned as one of the places where picture brides lived or where some of the women I helped came from. It was part of the broader region in California where Japanese immigrant communities faced challenges. I encountered women from such areas during my work with picture brides.

The Oriental School is mentioned in connection with the education of Japanese immigrant children. While I did not attend it myself, it was part of the community infrastructure supporting Japanese families in America. It was one of the institutions involved in the lives of the children and families I worked with.
